weight.title: Weight
weight.subtitle: Log your weight daily — the weekly average feeds your calorie target.
weight.empty: No data yet — log your first weight.

sedentary.label: Desk job, mostly sitting
sedentary.desc: Office work with little walking during the day.

lightly_active.label: Sitting + some walking
lightly_active.desc: Mostly seated, but on your feet a fair amount.

moderately_active.label: Mostly standing or walking
moderately_active.desc: On your feet most of the day (e.g. retail, teaching).

heavy.label: Physically demanding job
heavy.desc: Manual work most of the day (e.g. a tradesperson).

very_heavy.label: Very heavy labor
very_heavy.desc: Hard physical labor all day (e.g. construction).

formula.title: Mifflin-St Jeor formula
formula.intro: Your basal metabolic rate (BMR) is the energy your body uses at complete rest. We use the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, today's standard:

terms.weight: 10 × weight (kg) — more body mass burns more energy at rest.
terms.height: 6.25 × height (cm) — taller bodies have more tissue to maintain.
terms.age: − 5 × age — resting metabolism declines slowly with age.
terms.constant: +5 (men) / −161 (women) — a sex-based offset for average body composition.

formula.tdee: We multiply BMR by an occupational activity factor to get your maintenance need (TDEE) — the calories that keep your weight stable.
formula.goal: Your goal then shifts the target: cut = maintenance − 500 kcal, maintain = maintenance, bulk = maintenance + 300 kcal. A safety floor (1500 kcal for men, 1200 for women) prevents an extreme deficit.
formula.excludeTitle: Why exercise is not in the base
formula.exclude: The activity factor reflects your job and daily life only. Deliberate exercise and steps are added on top, per day, in a later phase — so a hard training day raises your budget for that day instead of inflating every day.

activity.title: Activity levels
activity.intro: Pick the option that best matches a typical day at work — not your workouts. Each level multiplies your BMR by the factor shown.

settings.title: Settings
settings.eatBackHint: Add steps/workout calories to your eating budget
settings.note: Imperial input arrives in a later phase; the engine always computes in metric.
